chapter1.title: Dawn at the Kitchen
chapter1.caption: Dawn at the Kitchen
chapter1.story: The first light catches the flour dust still hanging in the air from yesterday. Lena arrives at 4:15, flicks on the ovens, and stands in the quiet for exactly three minutes — the last silence she'll hear all day. By 4:30, the mixer is humming and the first batch of brown butter is already foaming on the stove.

chapter1.title: Weigh & Measure
chapter1.caption: Weigh & Measure
chapter1.story: We don't use cups here. Everything goes on the scale — 200 grams of dark brown sugar, 340 grams of European butter, each egg weighed to the gram. Marcus can scoop a kilo of flour and be off by less than five grams. He says his hands just know. Priya checks anyway.

chapter1.title: Mix & Fold
chapter1.caption: Mix & Fold
chapter1.story: The dough hook turns at exactly 42 RPM — fast enough to develop gluten, slow enough not to overheat the butter. Lena watches the dough pull away from the sides of the bowl, a moment she calls 'the clean break.' That's when she knows the structure is right. Too early and the cookies spread. Too late and they're tough. She's never been wrong.

chapter1.title: 48h Cold Rest
chapter1.caption: 48h Cold Rest
chapter1.story: This is where the magic actually happens — and nobody sees it. The dough rests in a walk-in fridge at 3°C for two full days. The flour fully hydrates, the butterfat recrystallizes, and enzymes quietly break starches into complex sugars. When people ask why our cookies taste different, the answer is in this fridge. Two days. No shortcuts.

chapter2.title: Oven Spring
chapter2.caption: Oven Spring
chapter2.story: The ovens run at exactly 200°C — not 195, not 205. Priya calibrated them herself with an infrared thermometer and checks the dials between every batch. When the dough balls hit the hot steel deck, they spread, then rise, then set — a 14-minute performance with three distinct acts. Through the glass door, you can watch the crinkle-top form. It never gets old.

chapter2.title: Bake to Order
chapter2.caption: Bake to Order
chapter2.story: Nothing sits in a display case here. When your order comes in, that's when your batch goes in. Marcus reads each ticket aloud — 'One dozen, heavy on the Double Chocolate' — and the team builds the box from scratch. The cookies you're eating were raw dough four hours ago. That's not a marketing line. That's the schedule.

chapter2.title: Quality Check
chapter2.caption: Quality Check
chapter2.story: Every batch gets a taste test. Not a visual check — an actual bite. Lena pulls one cookie from each tray, breaks it open, checks the crumb structure, and tastes it warm. If the salt doesn't hit right behind the chocolate, the whole batch gets pulled. 'We've done it twice,' she admits. 'Both times it hurt. Both times it was the right call.'

chapter2.title: Cooling Rack
chapter2.caption: Cooling Rack
chapter2.story: Fresh from the oven, a cookie is still cooking — residual heat keeps the center baking for another two minutes. The team transfers each tray to a wire rack with the gentleness of handling a newborn. Steam curls upward, carrying the smell of warm chocolate through the entire kitchen. Visitors on Open Day always stop here. Nobody blames them.

chapter3.title: Wrap & Pack
chapter3.caption: Wrap & Pack
chapter3.story: Each box is hand-folded from recycled kraft paper and stamped with the Big Cookies seal — a custom brass stamp Lena found at an estate sale in Sellwood. The tissue paper is compostable, the ribbon is cotton, and every box gets a handwritten note if you ask for one. The team can wrap and pack a dozen in under two minutes. They race each other. Priya holds the record.

chapter3.title: Ship Same Day
chapter3.caption: Ship Same Day
chapter3.story: By 2:00pm, the day's orders are boxed, labeled, and stacked by the loading door. Our courier, a guy named Eddie who's been doing this route for 16 years, backs his van up to the kitchen entrance and loads each box by hand. Portland orders arrive by dinner. Seattle by tomorrow. If your box shows up with a corner dent, Eddie probably already heard about it from Lena.

chapter3.title: You Enjoy
chapter3.caption: You Enjoy
chapter3.story: This is the moment the whole process builds toward. You open the box, the smell hits you first, and then you take a bite of something that started 48 hours ago as flour, butter, and an idea. Maybe you're on your couch. Maybe you're sharing with friends. Maybe you're hiding the last one so nobody else finds it. We get it. We do that too.

chapter3.title: Coffee Pairing
chapter3.caption: Coffee Pairing
chapter3.story: Every cookie has a soulmate. The Classic Chocolate Chip was practically designed for a dark roast — the bitterness cuts the sweetness and makes the chocolate taste deeper. Matcha White Chocolate wants green tea, obviously. Raspberry Dark Chocolate with a black coffee is a breakfast we fully endorse. Lena's personal pairing: Toffee Crunch with a flat white. 'Try it and tell me I'm wrong.'
